subject: History
chapter: Print Culture and the Modern World

activities_projects.title: The First Printed Books Collage
activities_projects.objective: Identify the earliest forms of printed materials
materials: Images of Diamond Sutra and Gutenberg Bible
steps: Collect pictures of ancient woodblock prints and the first printed Bible
Paste them on a chart
Label the country of origin (China/Germany) for each
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the transition from woodblock to mechanical printing
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of early prints (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Print Revolution Word Search
activities_projects.objective: Reinforce basic vocabulary related to printing
steps: Find words like: Gutenberg, Vellum, Calligraphy, Ballad, and Chapbook
Write a one-line definition for any three found words
activities_projects.evaluation: Completion and accuracy of definitions (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Penny Chapbook Designer
activities_projects.objective: Understand how books became affordable for the poor
steps: Fold an A4 sheet into a small 8-page booklet
Design a cover for a 'Penny Chapbook' (pocket-sized book)
Write a short folk tale or moral story inside
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Grasps the concept of 'cheap print' for mass literacy
activities_projects.evaluation: Creativity and relevance to the era (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Mapping the Spread of Print
activities_projects.objective: Trace the geographic journey of printing technology
steps: Mark the starting point in East Asia (China/Japan)
Trace the route to Europe via the Silk Route (Marco Polo)
Mark the location of the first printing press in India (Goa)
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Spatial understanding of how technology traveled globally
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of routes and dates (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Manuscript vs. Printed Book: A Dialogue
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the advantages and disadvantages of early printing
steps: Write a short dialogue between a 15th-century Scribe and a Printer
Discuss cost, speed, and the 'beauty' of calligraphy vs. identical printed copies
Highlight why some elites initially preferred manuscripts over printed books
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the resistance and shift to mechanical print
activities_projects.evaluation: Logical arguments and historical context (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Women’s Voices in Indian Print
activities_projects.objective: Examine the role of print in women's empowerment
steps: Research Rashsundari Devi and how she learned to read in secret
Write a summary of why books like 'Kailashbashini Debi's' were important in the 19th century
Discuss how print gave women a platform to share their lives
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes the link between literacy and social change
activities_projects.evaluation: Depth of research and clarity (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Protestant Reformation Audit
activities_projects.objective: Evaluate the link between print and religious upheaval
steps: Create a flowchart: Martin Luther’s writings -> Rapid printing -> Public debate -> The Protestant Reformation
Analyze Luther’s quote: 'Printing is the ultimate gift of God and the greatest one'
Explain the purpose of the Church's 'Index of Prohibited Books'
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Critical understanding of print as a tool for religious dissent
activities_projects.evaluation: Analytical depth and causal links (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Print and the French Revolution: A Critique
activities_projects.objective: Assess how print culture prepared the ground for revolution
steps: Research the three arguments linking print to the 1789 Revolution
Examine an 18th-century French caricature mocking the royalty
Write an essay debating if print 'caused' or 'reflected' public anger
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Ability to handle complex historiographical debates

activities_projects.title: Vernacular Press Act: A Mock News Debate
steps: Draft a news report covering the passing of the Vernacular Press Act (1878)
Include an interview with a British official and an Indian editor
Explain the 'model' of the Irish Press Laws used in India
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Grasps the conflict between colonial authority and the free press

activities_projects.title: The Gutenberg Legacy Simulation
activities_projects.objective: Calculate the impact of 'Movable Type' on knowledge production
steps: Task 1: Manually copy a 100-word text by hand. Record the time.
Task 2: Use stamps to 'print' the same text. Record the time.
Analyze how 'mass production' changed the price of books and created a 'Reading Public'
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Quantitative understanding of printing press efficiency
